title: Slow-roast beef brisket
subtitle: Four hours low and slow until sliceable and yielding
excerpt: Beef brisket braised in stock and red wine at a low temperature for four hours until it slices cleanly but falls apart at the edges. A cheaper cut that rewards patience.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Boiled and braised beef methods.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Pot-roasted and braised beef.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced brisket covered in its cooking juices for up to three months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook a day ahead and refrigerate. The fat will solidify on top and can be lifted off cleanly. Reheat covered at 150 degrees C for 45 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking a large piece of meat slowly in a covered pot with a small amount of liquid, so it cooks in a combination of liquid and its own steam.
Cooking at a low temperature for several hours, allowing the collagen in tougher cuts to convert to gelatin, producing a moist and tender result.

content.text: Brisket is a working muscle with a high collagen content that makes it tough when cooked quickly and yielding when cooked slowly. Four hours at 150 degrees C in a covered casserole with stock and red wine converts the collagen to gelatin, producing meat that slices cleanly and collapses at the edges. It is the best value roasting beef available.

items.prepNote: rolled and tied, or flat unrolled

content.text: Heat the oven to 150 degrees C fan. Pat the brisket dry and season generously with salt. Heat the oil in a casserole over high heat and sear the brisket on all sides until deeply browned, about 3 to 4 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the onion, carrot, and celery to the casserole and cook for 5 minutes until starting to soften. Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute more.
Pour in the red wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the hot stock, thyme, and bay leaves. Return the brisket on top of the vegetables, fat-side up.
in the oven for 4 hours. Check at 2 hours and add a splash of stock or water if the liquid level has dropped significantly.
The brisket is ready when it
to full tenderness: a skewer should pass through with no resistance. Transfer to a board and rest for 20 minutes. Strain the cooking juices and serve alongside.

items.symptom: The brisket is still very tough after 4 hours
items.cause: A cold joint went in without searing, or the casserole lid was not tight.
items.fix: Continue cooking: brisket can take up to 5 hours. Ensure the lid seals properly. If it does not, place a sheet of greaseproof paper under the lid.

items.symptom: The cooking liquid has reduced to a sticky mass and is threatening to burn
items.cause: Not enough liquid was added, or the casserole was not tightly covered.
items.fix: Check at 2 hours and add more hot stock if needed. The liquid level should sit about halfway up the meat.

items.symptom: The meat falls apart completely and cannot be sliced
items.fix: Pull the brisket apart with two forks and serve as pulled beef. This is not a failure: pulled brisket is excellent.

items.symptom: The cooking juices are thin and lack flavour
items.cause: The searing step was skipped, removing the fond (browned residue) that builds flavour.
items.fix: Always sear before braising. Reduce the strained juices on the hob over medium heat for 10 minutes to concentrate.

content.text: Add a tablespoon of tomato puree with the wine for a richer, darker sauce. A few anchovy fillets dissolved into the vegetables before the wine adds depth without a fishy flavour. For a simpler weekday version, skip the searing and add everything cold to the casserole: the flavour is less complex but the method is entirely hands-off.

Pot-roasted brisket is a working-class British and Jewish cooking tradition. The brisket was the affordable cut that tough-cooked into a tender Sunday meal. Food writer Eliza Acton and Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton both described similar slow-braised beef methods. Brisket fell out of favour when cheaper cuts became available. It has returned through barbecue culture and renewed interest in economical cooking.
